title: Set the Night on FIRE: Building an Empirical Local Dark Matter Velocity Distribution
comment: 22 pages, 18 figures. To be submitted to ApJ
summary: The majority of terrestrial direct detection experiments for Dark Matter (DM) rely on the Standard Halo Model (SHM), which assumes the local DM velocity distribution follows a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution. However, galaxy mergers can deposit DM that remains kinematically clustered today, inducing deviations from the smooth SHM prediction. Previous studies have suggested that the local stellar velocity distribution may serve as a tracer for DM populations originating from the same progenitor systems. In this work, we systematically investigate how merger mass and accretion time affect the correlation between local stellar and DM velocity distributions in Milky Way-like galaxies from the FIRE-2 simulations. We find a strong correlation between traceable DM components and their stellar counterparts, with the tightest correspondence arising from lower-mass mergers accreted at earlier cosmic times. For the remaining DM that lacks an identifiable stellar counterpart, which dominate the full DM fraction, we find that its velocity distribution is well described by a component-wise generalized Gaussian. Combining these two ingredients, we reconstruct the full local DM velocity distribution. This framework captures merger-induced features-such as co-rotation of accreted material with the galactic disk-that are entirely absent in the SHM. Finally, we propagate uncertainties through the reconstruction and show that they are dominated by the stellar mass-halo mass relation, which is unlikely to improve substantially in the near term. We therefore argue that this framework approaches the current limit of our ability to characterize the local DM velocity distribution.

AI.tldr: A framework using stellar velocity distributions as tracers for dark matter from mergers, combined with generalized Gaussian components for untraceable DM, reconstructs the local DM velocity distribution with features absent in the Standard Halo Model.
AI.motivation: The Standard Halo Model assumes a smooth Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution for local DM velocities, but galaxy mergers can deposit kinematically clustered DM that deviates from this prediction. Since stellar velocity distributions may trace DM from the same progenitor systems, there's a need to systematically investigate this correlation and develop a more accurate reconstruction framework.
AI.method: Systematically investigated how merger mass and accretion time affect correlation between local stellar and DM velocity distributions using Milky Way-like galaxies from FIRE-2 simulations. Used stellar velocity distributions as tracers for DM components from mergers, and for untraceable DM (which dominates the total), applied component-wise generalized Gaussian distributions.
AI.result: Found strong correlation between traceable DM components and their stellar counterparts, with tightest correspondence from lower-mass mergers accreted earlier. Untraceable DM velocity distribution is well described by component-wise generalized Gaussian. Combined these to reconstruct full local DM velocity distribution, capturing merger-induced features like co-rotation with galactic disk that are absent in SHM. Reconstruction uncertainties dominated by stellar mass-halo mass relation.
AI.conclusion: This framework approaches the current limit of our ability to characterize the local DM velocity distribution, as uncertainties are dominated by the stellar mass-halo mass relation which is unlikely to improve substantially in the near term. The method captures important merger-induced features missing from the Standard Halo Model.

title: The Hubble Missing Globular Cluster Survey. III. Astro-photometric catalogs, artificial-star tests, and improved absolute proper motions
comment: 12 pages, 9 figures, 6 tables. Accepted for publication in A&A. Catalogs available upon publication
summary: The Hubble Missing Globular Cluster Survey (MGCS) has taken one of the last opportunities to complete the census of Galactic globular clusters (GCs) started by past Hubble Space Telescope (HST) programs, securing high-resolution data for 34 GCs never observed before by HST. The previous papers of the series have highlighted the astrometric and photometric potential of the project by analyzing a subsample of targets. We present, and release to the community, the official astro-photometric catalogs of the MGCS for all GCs imaged by this project. We describe the data reduction using state-of-the-art techniques designed for HST. We discuss the photometric calibration and show, for the first time, the synergy with the Gaia catalog to ensure homogeneous photometry across our data set. We compute artificial-star tests that can be used to assess systematics and the completeness level of our data. We combined HST and Gaia data to refine the absolute PMs of our GCs, reaching a precision $\sim$3 times better than that of Gaia alone. We used these new PMs to update (and to determine for the first time for six systems) the associations between GCs and their putative galaxy progenitors. This work continues decades-long efforts of large Treasury programs in sharing precise and accurate atlases to the community for studying GCs across a wide range of scientific endeavors.

AI.tldr: The MGCS project completes the Hubble census of Galactic globular clusters, providing high-resolution data for 34 previously unobserved GCs, releasing comprehensive astro-photometric catalogs with refined proper motions and progenitor galaxy associations.
AI.motivation: To complete the census of Galactic globular clusters started by past HST programs by securing high-resolution data for 34 GCs never observed before by HST, and to provide precise astro-photometric catalogs to the community for scientific research.
AI.method: Used state-of-the-art HST data reduction techniques, performed photometric calibration with Gaia synergy, conducted artificial-star tests for systematic assessment, and combined HST and Gaia data to refine absolute proper motions with ~3× better precision than Gaia alone.
AI.result: Released official astro-photometric catalogs for all MGCS-imaged GCs, achieved proper motion precision ~3 times better than Gaia alone, updated associations between GCs and their putative galaxy progenitors, and determined these associations for six systems for the first time.
AI.conclusion: This work represents a significant contribution to globular cluster research by completing the HST census, providing high-precision data products to the community, and enabling improved understanding of GC origins and galaxy formation through refined proper motions and progenitor associations.

title: The YREC Stellar Evolution Code: Public Data Release
comment: 37 pages, 22 figures. ApJ in press. The code repository for the release paper can be found at https://github.com/yreclab/yrec/tree/yrec_2026_release
summary: In this paper we present the public release of the Yale Rotating Evolution Code (YREC). YREC is a stellar evolution code that covers brown dwarfs and stars across a wide range of masses, and evolutionary states from the pre-MS through helium burning. We summarize the key ingredients of the code, document the code performance, and discuss its strengths and limitations. We present libraries of input files, documentation, sample use cases, and scripts. In addition to usage as a research tool, we highlight the utility of the code for educational purposes.

AI.tldr: YREC is a publicly released stellar evolution code for brown dwarfs and stars across wide mass ranges and evolutionary states, now available with documentation, libraries, and educational utilities.
AI.motivation: To make a comprehensive stellar evolution code publicly available for research and educational use, covering a wide range of stellar objects and evolutionary phases.
AI.method: Public release of the Yale Rotating Evolution Code (YREC) with accompanying documentation, input file libraries, sample use cases, and scripts.
AI.result: YREC is now available as a research tool capable of modeling brown dwarfs and stars from pre-main sequence through helium burning, with documented performance and limitations.
AI.conclusion: YREC provides a valuable publicly accessible stellar evolution code suitable for both research applications and educational purposes.

title: Revisiting the Evidence for Double Sequences of Blue Straggler Stars in Globular Clusters
comment: Accepted for publication in The Astronomical Journal (AJ). 19 pages, 9 figures
summary: Blue straggler stars (BSSs) are believed to form through mass transfer in binary systems or stellar collisions. The reported presence of double BSS sequences in some globular clusters (GCs) has been interpreted as evidence that these two formation channels produce distinct sequences in color-magnitude diagram (CMD). We reassess this claim using HST UV Globular Cluster Survey (HUGS) photometry of 56 Galactic GCs. We used the Hartigan Dip Test to test bimodality, and Akaike model comparison to test whether BSS distance distributions are better described by a mixture of two unskewed Gaussians or a skewed unimodal Gaussian model. We find no strong statistical evidence for bimodality; no cluster yields a dip test p-value below 0.15, and Akaike model comparison favors the skewed unimodal model in 94 out of 112 cases. We re-examine NGC 7099 (M30), the prototypical case of a double BSS sequence, using three reductions of HST data. We find bimodality is detected at p = 4 x 10-3 , vs. the originally reported p ~ 10-5 , in the original photometry. The observed uncertainties derived from the subgiant branch widths are comparable to the suggested separation between the proposed BSS sequences, making the detection of statistically significant bimodality challenging. Our results suggest that the dip between two BSS sequences in M30 photometry is a coincidence, and that later bifurcation claims can be explained as skew in the BSS color distribution, rather than two separate distributions.

AI.tldr: Reanalysis of blue straggler star distributions in 56 globular clusters finds no strong statistical evidence for bimodality, challenging previous claims of double sequences from different formation channels.
AI.motivation: Previous studies reported double BSS sequences in some globular clusters, interpreted as evidence for distinct formation channels (mass transfer vs. stellar collisions). This study reassesses these claims using more comprehensive data and rigorous statistical methods.
AI.method: Used HST UV Globular Cluster Survey (HUGS) photometry of 56 Galactic GCs; applied Hartigan Dip Test for bimodality testing and Akaike model comparison between two unskewed Gaussians (bimodal) vs. skewed unimodal Gaussian models; re-examined NGC 7099 (M30) with three HST data reductions.
AI.result: No strong statistical evidence for bimodality found: no cluster had dip test p-value below 0.15; Akaike comparison favored skewed unimodal model in 94/112 cases; for M30, bimodality detected at p=4×10⁻³ vs. originally reported p~10⁻⁵; observed uncertainties comparable to suggested sequence separation.
AI.conclusion: The dip between two BSS sequences in M30 photometry is likely a coincidence; later bifurcation claims can be explained by skew in BSS color distributions rather than separate distributions; no compelling evidence for distinct BSS sequences from different formation channels.

title: Dynamical Age of Alfvénic Turbulence in the Solar Wind
summary: An evolving turbulent flow such as the solar wind can be meaningfully characterized by its "turbulence age" -- an estimate of the number of nonlinear times that have elapsed during a plasma parcel's propagation from the Sun to a given point in space. Recent observations of the near-Sun solar wind by the \textit{Parker Solar Probe} (\textit{PSP}) indicate high correlation between velocity and magnetic fluctuations (i.e., cross helicity, $σ_c$), which is known to impede development of magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) turbulence. Here we propose a new formulation of the turbulence age ($A_\text{t}$) of the solar wind that explicitly accounts for the Alfvénic nature of the fluctuations in the inner heliosphere. $A_\text{t}$ is then evaluated for slow and fast wind streams using a variety of data sources -- observations from the \textit{PSP, Advanced Composition Explorer}, and \textit{Voyager} missions, and a global solar wind simulation that includes turbulence transport. Compared to the formulation employed in previous work that neglected Alfvénicity, the present approach yields smaller values of $A_\text{t}$ in medium-to-high $σ_c$ solar wind; similar turbulence ages are then obtained for slow and fast wind in the ecliptic. The radial evolution of $A_\text{t}$ between heliocentric distances of $r\sim 0.2$ to 40 AU is examined. The rate of increase of $A_\text{t}$ is found to decrease until $\sim 5$ AU, indicating a gradual slowing of the \textit{in situ} development of turbulence in the inner heliosphere. Beyond $\sim 5$ AU this rate begins to increase, likely due to turbulence driving by pick-up ions. This paper highlights the important role of cross helicity in modulating MHD turbulence, and the results will aid in further interpretations of observations of the radial evolution of various turbulence parameters in the solar wind.

AI.tldr: A new turbulence age formulation accounts for Alfvénic fluctuations in solar wind, showing slower turbulence development in inner heliosphere and modulation by cross helicity.
AI.motivation: Recent Parker Solar Probe observations show high correlation between velocity and magnetic fluctuations (cross helicity) in near-Sun solar wind, which impedes MHD turbulence development. Previous turbulence age formulations neglected this Alfvénic nature.
AI.method: Proposed new turbulence age formulation (A_t) that explicitly accounts for Alfvénic fluctuations. Evaluated using multi-source data: Parker Solar Probe, Advanced Composition Explorer, Voyager observations, and global solar wind simulation with turbulence transport.
AI.result: New formulation yields smaller turbulence ages in medium-to-high cross helicity solar wind compared to previous approach. Similar turbulence ages obtained for slow and fast wind in ecliptic. Rate of A_t increase decreases until ~5 AU (slowing turbulence development), then increases beyond 5 AU likely due to pick-up ion turbulence driving.
AI.conclusion: Cross helicity plays important role in modulating MHD turbulence. New formulation aids interpretation of radial evolution of turbulence parameters in solar wind from 0.2 to 40 AU.

title: Direct evidence for magnetohydrodynamic disk winds driving rotating outflows in protostar HOPS 358
comment: 5 main figures, 4 supplementary figures, and 1 supplementary table, accepted for publication in Nature Communications (in press)
summary: Angular momentum removal is a fundamental requirement for star and planet formation, yet the mechanisms driving this process remain debated. Magnetohydrodynamic disk winds, launched along magnetic field lines from extended disk regions, offer a promising solution, particularly in regions where magnetorotational turbulence is weak. Here we present high-resolution Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array observations of the Class 0 protostar HOPS 358, revealing a rotating, nested outflow structure traced by H2CO, SO, and CH3OH emission. The outflow preserves the disk's rotational sense and is aligned with the disk axis, providing direct observational evidence for a magnetically launched disk wind. From the measured kinematics, we derive a dimensionless magnetic lever arm of approximately 2.3 and constrain the wind-launching region to radii of 10-18 astronomical units within the planet-forming zone. These results demonstrate that magnetohydrodynamic disk winds operate during the deeply embedded phase, efficiently extracting angular momentum while shaping disk evolution and establishing initial conditions for planet formation.

AI.tldr: ALMA observations of protostar HOPS 358 reveal a rotating, nested outflow structure aligned with the disk axis, providing direct evidence for magnetically launched disk winds that remove angular momentum during early star formation.
AI.motivation: Angular momentum removal is essential for star and planet formation, but the mechanisms driving this process remain debated. Magnetohydrodynamic disk winds offer a promising solution, especially in regions where magnetorotational turbulence is weak, but direct observational evidence has been lacking.
AI.method: High-resolution ALMA observations of the Class 0 protostar HOPS 358, analyzing H2CO, SO, and CH3OH emission to trace the outflow structure and kinematics. The study examines the outflow's rotation, alignment with the disk axis, and preservation of the disk's rotational sense.
AI.result: The observations reveal a rotating, nested outflow structure that preserves the disk's rotational sense and is aligned with the disk axis, providing direct evidence for a magnetically launched disk wind. From kinematics, the dimensionless magnetic lever arm is approximately 2.3, and the wind-launching region is constrained to radii of 10-18 AU within the planet-forming zone.
AI.conclusion: Magnetohydrodynamic disk winds operate during the deeply embedded phase of star formation, efficiently extracting angular momentum while shaping disk evolution and establishing initial conditions for planet formation. These winds provide a fundamental mechanism for angular momentum removal in protoplanetary disks.

title: Population III star formation in an X-ray background: V. Environmental dependence and halo occupation probability
summary: An X-ray background in the early Universe enhances molecular hydrogen formation, the main coolant of primordial gas, thereby lowering the threshold for Pop III star formation. Continuing our series on X-ray impacts on Pop III star formation, we investigate how a soft X-ray background promotes Pop III star formation using cosmological zoom-in simulations of ten cosmic volumes spanning a range of halo number densities. Each volume is irradiated by the Lyman-Warner (LW) H$_{2}$ dissociating background and a weak (J$_{21} \sim 10^{-5}$), soft ($E \sim 0.2-2.0$ keV) X-ray background produced by pair-instability SNe (PISNe) from Pop III stars and calculated self-consistently as described in a companion paper. We also compare the same simulations with and without X-rays to isolate the X-ray effect. The background promotes Pop III star formation in two ways: (1) by reducing the mean host halo mass by a factor of $\sim 2-3$, and (2) by enabling Pop III star formation in haloes that would otherwise remain sterile, thereby increasing the halo occupation fraction. The resulting gain in Pop III number density is largest in underdense regions (a factor of $\approx 3$ on average, reaching up to 7). In the most extreme case, Pop II stars form only in the presence of X-rays and the gas-phase metallicity rises by an order of magnitude, suggesting that dwarf galaxies in underdense regions may be significantly influenced by an early X-ray background. We also provide fitting functions for the halo occupation probability of Pop III stars as a function of redshift for both X-ray and LW-only simulations, which can serve as inputs for semi-analytic models.

AI.tldr: Soft X-ray background from Pop III supernovae enhances molecular hydrogen formation, lowering the mass threshold for Pop III star formation and increasing their number density, especially in underdense regions.
AI.motivation: To investigate how a soft X-ray background produced by pair-instability supernovae from Pop III stars affects Pop III star formation in the early Universe, particularly in different cosmic density environments.
AI.method: Used cosmological zoom-in simulations of ten cosmic volumes with varying halo number densities, comparing simulations with and without X-rays. Simulations included Lyman-Werner H₂ dissociating background and a weak, soft X-ray background (J₂₁ ∼ 10⁻⁵, E ∼ 0.2-2.0 keV) from Pop III pair-instability supernovae.
AI.result: X-ray background promotes Pop III star formation by: (1) reducing mean host halo mass by factor ∼2-3, (2) enabling star formation in otherwise sterile haloes, increasing halo occupation fraction. Pop III number density gain largest in underdense regions (average factor ≈3, up to 7). In extreme cases, Pop II stars form only with X-rays, and gas-phase metallicity rises by order of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: Early soft X-ray background significantly enhances Pop III star formation, particularly in underdense regions, potentially influencing dwarf galaxy formation. Provided fitting functions for halo occupation probability can inform semi-analytic models of early star formation.
